---
title: "Kelvin Wong"
image: profile.jpg
about:
  template: trestles
  image-shape: round
  image-width: 15em
  links:
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: https://github.com/kelvin1189
    - icon: linkedin
      text: LinkedIn
      href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/[YOUR-LINKEDIN-SLUG]
---

## About me

I'm Malaysian by birth, did my graduate work in Taiwan, and have stayed there since. I'm a senior specialist based in Taiwan working on life insurance risk management — valuation, ALM, and the modeling that underpins both. This blog is where I work through ideas adjacent to that work, usually with code, usually using public market data.

## About this blog

The blog opens with a multi-article series on the Dynamic Nelson-Siegel interest rate model under the IAIS Insurance Capital Standard, applied to TWD government bond data. That choice isn't accidental — it's a topic I'm thinking about in my work, and writing about it publicly forces me to be explicit about my reasoning in a way that internal documents do not.

I want this site to function less as polished publication and more as a working notebook: a record of how I think through a problem, what I tried and discarded, where I'm still uncertain. Posts will sometimes be longer and more exploratory than typical technical blog posts as a result. The hope is that readers can see the actual decision points, not only the cleaned-up answer.

The recurring themes are actuarial and quantitative — yield-curve modeling, insurance capital frameworks, risk methods — but the notebook framing means I may occasionally write on adjacent topics where the same modes of thinking apply. I'd rather follow a question where it leads than confine the blog to a strict scope.

Everything published here is my own opinion and does not represent the views of any current or past employer.

## Background

I hold an M.Sc. in Risk Management and Insurance from Taiwan and an undergraduate degree in Mathematics and Physics. I'm an SOA candidate. I have experience across life and P&C insurance capital and risk modeling, and I work primarily in R, Python, and SQL.

## A note on AI assistance

Throughout the work on this blog I make active use of large language models — principally Claude (Anthropic), Gemini (Google), and Grok (xAI) — for English revision, code debugging, document structuring, and as a sparring partner when I want to stress-test an argument before committing it to a post.

I should be honest about a second use case as well. I had wanted to start a blog like this for nearly a decade, and repeatedly got stuck not on the substance but on the publishing stack itself: choosing between Jekyll, Hugo, and Quarto; learning Git workflows for content rather than code; building a site design that looked credible without a frontend background. These are real skills, and none of them are taught by a quantitative or actuarial education. AI tools let me work through that stack quickly enough to actually get a site live, which I had not previously been able to do on my own.

What stays mine: the choice of topics, the modeling decisions, the interpretation of results, and the conclusions. The AI tools help me express that content more clearly than I could on my own in a second language, and they help me ship the wrapper around the content. Any errors that remain in published posts are also mine.

I mention this for transparency, and because the question of how to use these tools well in technical writing seems worth being open about rather than quietly working around.

## Get in touch

The fastest ways to reach me are GitHub (for code or blog corrections) or LinkedIn (for everything else). For technical errors in posts, opening a GitHub issue against the [blog repo](https://github.com/kelvin1189/actuarial-modeling-notes) is the most efficient route.
